result: The quietest sound in the universe is the click of a closing book at 3 a.m., when the house itself seems to be holding its breath. I’ve been chasing that hush since childhood, when I first realized that words could outlive their authors and that dragons could fit between two paperback covers. Tonight the chase ends in a library built inside a decommissioned lighthouse; every shelf spirals upward like a corkscrew of time. I climb with a penlight between my teeth, past atlases whose coasts have melted, past love letters carbon-dated to the Ice Age. Halfway up I meet the ghost of the original keeper; he smells of kerosene and salt. He offers to trade me one unread page for every year I have left. I laugh—what’s mortality compared to an unopened chapter? Still, my hand trembles as I tear out the flyleaf of a water-warped novel and write my own name in the margin, backdating myself into a story that hasn’t happened yet. The ink glows, a bioluminescent confession. Outside, the tide erases footprints neither of us made, and the beam, long dark, suddenly flares once, twice, like a wink from a distant god who’s also been skimming ahead. I close the book; the click echoes down the spiral, ricocheting off centuries until it lands somewhere behind my sternum, small and bright as a star caught in a jar. I descend lighter by exactly one life, unsure whether I’m returning or just beginning the climb.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
